a:[["$","meta","0",{"name":"viewport","content":"width=device-width, initial-scale=1"}],["$","meta","1",{"charSet":"utf-8"}],["$","title","2",{"children":"IB Revision Bank"}],["$","meta","3",{"name":"description","content":"Free, curated IB question bank covering Mathematics (AA & AI), Physics, Chemistry, Biology, Economics and ESS — every question grouped by syllabus topic and pulled from public revision sources."}]]

b:T117fb,<div class="specification">
<p>An investigation was carried out to examine whether honeybees (<em>Apis mellifera</em>) can learn “rules” to deal with complex tasks and then apply them to new situations. To train bees, they were rewarded each time they followed a cue to make a correct turn at the start of a training maze. They were then allowed to find their way through the entire training maze.</p>
<p style="text-align: center;"><img src="data:image/png;base64,"/></p>
<p style="text-align: left;">This was followed by attempts to navigate two unfamiliar mazes (maze 1 and maze 2) to see whether the bees could apply the rules to follow a path through different mazes. Untrained control bees were also put into mazes. Only one bee was tested in each maze at a time.</p>
<p style="text-align: left;">The bees were classified according to how successful they were in making their way through each maze. The results are shown in the bar chart.</p>
<p style="text-align: center;"><img src="data:image/png;base64,"/></p>
<p style="text-align: center;">[Source: reprinted from <em>Neurobiology of Learning and Memory</em>, <strong>72</strong>, S.W. Zhang <em>et al</em>, Honeybee Memory: Navigation by<br/>Associative Grouping and Recall of Visual Stimuli, 180–201, Copyright 1999, with permission from Elsevier]</p>
</div><div class="question" style="padding-left: 20px; padding-right: 20px;">
<p>Distinguish between learned behaviour and innate behaviour.</p>
<div class="marks">[2]</div>
<div class="question_part_label">a.</div>
</div><div class="question" style="padding-left: 20px; padding-right: 20px;">
<p>Evaluate how the data supports the hypothesis that honeybees are able to learn to use cues to find the path through new mazes.</p>
<div class="marks">[3]</div>
<div class="question_part_label">b.i.</div>
</div><div class="question" style="padding-left: 20px; padding-right: 20px;">
<p>Suggest an advantage of such behaviour.</p>
<div class="marks">[1]</div>
<div class="question_part_label">b.ii.</div>
</div>c:T129b5,<div class="question" style="padding-left: 20px;">
<p><img src="data:image/png;base64,"/></p>
<p><em>Not necessary to answer within a table.</em></p>
<div class="question_part_label">a.</div>
</div><div class="question" style="padding-left: 20px;">
<p>a. trained bees made few mistakes/were successful ✔</p>
<p>b. trained bees could use cues on two different mazes so able to remember «overtime»<br/><em><strong>OR</strong></em><br/>bees who did well on training maze continued to do well on other mazes ✔</p>
<p>c. «approximately» same percentage/frequency of successful bees on training and other two mazes ✔</p>
<p>d. most control/untrained bees unable to find path through maze without mistakes<br/><em><strong>OR</strong></em><br/>most control/untrained bees always made mistakes ✔</p>
<p><img src="data:image/png;base64,"/></p>
<p style="text-align: center;">[Source: reprinted from <em>Neurobiology of Learning and Memory</em>, <strong>72</strong>, S.W. Zhang <em>et al</em>, Honeybee Memory: Navigation by Associative Grouping and Recall of Visual Stimuli, 180–201, Copyright 1999, with permission from Elsevier]</p>
<p><em>Accept converse statements.</em></p>
<div class="question_part_label">b.i.</div>
</div><div class="question" style="padding-left: 20px;">
<p>a. return to flowers with nectar<br/><em><strong>OR</strong></em><br/>«more chance» to obtain food ✔</p>
<p>b. increases chances of survival «if they can learn directions to and from food» ✔</p>
<div class="question_part_label">b.ii.</div>
</div>2:["$","article",null,{"className":"q-page","children":[["$","p",null,{"style":{"margin":0},"children":["$","$L6",null,{"href":"/subject/biology/topic/a/","children":["← back to ","Biology"," topic ","a"]}]}],["$","h1",null,{"children":"19M.3.HL.TZ2.5"}],["$","div",null,{"className":"q-meta","children":[["$","span",null,{"className":"tag","children":"pestle"}],["$","span",null,{"children":"Biology"}],null,["$","span",null,{"children":"HL"}],["$","span",null,{"children":["Paper ","3"]}],["$","span",null,{"children":"19M"}],["$","span",null,{"children":["· ","a-4-innate-and-learned-behaviour"]}],["$","a",null,{"href":"https://pestle.pages.dev/app/","target":"_blank","rel":"noopener","children":"source ↗"}]]}],["$","div",null,{"className":"q-content","dangerouslySetInnerHTML":{"__html":"$b"}}],["$","details",null,{"className":"solution","children":[["$","summary",null,{"children":"Markscheme / solution"}],["$","div",null,{"dangerouslySetInnerHTML":{"__html":"$c"}}]]}],["$","details",null,{"className":"solution","children":[["$","summary",null,{"children":"Examiners’ report"}],["$","div",null,{"dangerouslySetInnerHTML":{"__html":"<div class=\"question\" style=\"padding-left: 20px;\">\n<p>Most produced correct answers, but some had difficulties pairing distinctions of the same type.</p>\n<div class=\"question_part_label\">a.</div>\n</div><div class=\"question\" style=\"padding-left: 20px;\">\n<p>Most did well on evaluating the data, but it was more difficult for some to suggest an advantage of the behaviour and apply it to the bees.</p>\n<div class=\"question_part_label\">b.i.</div>\n</div><div class=\"question\" style=\"padding-left: 20px;\">\n<p>Most did well on evaluating the data, but it was more difficult for some to suggest an advantage of the behaviour and apply it to the bees.</p>\n<div class=\"question_part_label\">b.ii.</div>\n</div>"}}]]}]]}]
